decomposition.title: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing, enterprise IT infrastructure, hosting/cloud/DCaaS, and digital operations
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget stated. Inferred approximately $15M-$50M over 3-5 years for the potential future hosting/DC exit program based on enterprise-scale Wisconsin data center transition, SAP ecosystem, VMware/physical server estate, Windows/Linux VMs, multiple databases, backup/DR, network connectivity, monitoring/logging/security tooling, and pricing for foundational setup, hosting, migration, support, and managed services. Phase 0 evaluation-only advisory effort would likely be materially smaller.
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated. Inferred likely 3-5 year hosting/transformation commercial horizon after Phase 0, with the RFP evaluation timeline running from 29 May 2026 through supplier presentations in the week of 6 July 2026.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must address environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must address identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must address the hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must address region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency appears as one bullet inside the Scope of Work rather than as a detailed compliance section; it is easy to under-answer if the vendor focuses only on architecture.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach required, but specific jurisdictions are not defined in the provided document.

requirements.text: Vendors must address disaster recovery, high availability, and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs, and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios (Cloud, DCaaS, Hybrid) where applicable and clearly state assumptions.

In-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center, commercial off-the-shelf and internally developed applications, Windows and Linux virtual machines, databases, middleware, file storage, backup and disaster recovery, identity integrations, network connectivity, monitoring, logging, security tooling, and selected OT-adjacent workloads limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
Database scope includes SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL, and other supported platforms.
Selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope only for read-only or controlled integration scenarios.

Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset, highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information, and use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The detailed inventory is an attachment outside the main document; inaccurate use of the inventory could make sizing and pricing non-comparable.

requirements.text: Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Certification evidence is mandatory but not listed in the summary; details may be hidden in the Vendor Response Template referenced by the RFP.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not enumerated.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required; specific certifications not named in the main document.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a provider-type-specific mandatory requirement embedded in the Compliance & Regulatory Requirements section and points to the Vendor Response Template for more detail.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers.

requirements.text: Execution of security controls, compliance certification, or regulatory audits is out of scope; vendors are only required to demonstrate capability and readiness.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP excludes execution of controls/audits but still requires capability/readiness demonstration; vendors may over- or under-scope compliance work.

requirements.text: Responses are expected to support solution feasibility, approach, and business case development, not detailed implementation commitments, because the RFP is part of a Phase 0 evaluation exercise.
Vendors are required to submit structured, complete, and comparable proposals aligned to the requirements outlined in this RFP and the associated response templates.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Completeness and alignment requirements are in response instructions; non-compliant formatting can cause rejection before substantive evaluation.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Submission deadline/channel requirements are easy to treat as administrative, but they govern whether a proposal is accepted for evaluation.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The mandatory response template is an external attachment; failure to complete it in detail or using simple Yes/No answers may make the bid non-responsive.

requirements.text: All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided below.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification process requires use of a template and deadline compliance.

requirements.text: All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The designated communication path includes a named individual and copy mailbox; using an alternate route may create submission or communication risk.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured, and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.

Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup, including foundational setup costs, application and infrastructure hosting costs, migration/implementation costs if applicable, and any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: SAP/non-SAP cost separation is a specific commercial formatting requirement buried in pricing guidance.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency compliance is a simple administrative requirement that can make commercial submissions non-comparable if missed.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
Payment terms are an important part of the proposal; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Net 90 appears in payment terms rather than headline eligibility; it is commercially important but not explicitly stated as an automatic rejection criterion.

requirements.text: Vendors will be evaluated across target state architecture, security and governance capabilities; migration approach, risk considerations and transition strategy; networking and connectivity; availability, resilience and disaster recovery; cost and commercial transparency; operating model and support capabilities; tools, ecosystem and platform maturity; and alignment to expected deliverables.

Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model, including indicative governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework, and high-level roles and responsibilities.
Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This mandatory confidentiality use restriction is in the Legal & Compliance Appendix, a common hiding spot for disqualifiers.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Third-party sharing restrictions in the appendix can affect subcontractor, hyperscaler, offshore, and partner review workflows.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This broad compliance capability requirement is repeated in the appendix and may become more specific in later legal terms.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applies to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments; exact standards and data protection obligations are not enumerated.

No explicit budget or contract value is stated. Deal size is inferred from the stated enterprise-scale hosting/DC exit scope, SAP and non-SAP estate, VMware/physical footprint, DR, network, security, monitoring, and pricing requirements.
No explicit contract term is stated. Duration is inferred from typical enterprise hosting transformation/commercial terms and the Phase 0 evaluation timeline.
Compliance certifications are required, but the specific certification names are not listed in the main document; vendors must consult the Vendor Response Template referenced on page 6.
The document says anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, but it does not explicitly state automatic rejection.

The RFP is a Phase 0 evaluation and states it is not a commitment to a specific architecture or execution approach, while pricing guidelines request hosting, migration/implementation, consulting, support, and managed services cost views; the intended commercial award scope is therefore not fully defined in the main RFP.

DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards. [PAGE 7]
Region strategy and data residency approach must be addressed in the solution. [PAGE 6]

similar_deals.title: Kohler Co. — Project Polaris Hosting / DCaaS / Hybrid Evaluation
similar_deals.industry: Manufacturing / enterprise IT infrastructure / hosting / cloud / DCaaS
similar_deals.deal_size_usd: Phase 0 advisory evidence range $279,533-$595,000; downstream hosting/DCaaS/hybrid opportunity inferred at $15M-$50M+ over 3-5 years, with one generated model at $30,667,392
similar_deals.outcome: No win/loss outcome available in provided intelligence; treated as pursuit-specific proposal intelligence, not proof of win.
similar_deals.why_relevant: Exact client, title, industry, geography and scope match. Provides the most directly reusable positioning, delivery methodology and pricing bands for the current RFP.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Use a vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, workload-by-workload placement model, USD-only open-book TCO, landing-zone blueprint, transition sequencing, rollback gates, shared-responsibility RACI, vendor coexistence, resiliency tiers and FinOps guardrails.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout
similar_deals.why_relevant: Strong manufacturing, SAP ecosystem, global/USA footprint and enterprise transformation relevance. Useful for SAP hosting, low-disruption migration and manufacturing continuity themes.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Executive sponsor alignment and strong SAP vertical references were decisive; BMW case study and SAP S/4HANA RISE capability differentiated the bid.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Very close commercial scale and duration to the inferred $15M-$50M hosting/DC exit program. Highly relevant because the Kohler RFP has eliminatory compliance and proposal-completeness requirements.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Missed local entity/compliance appendix requirement caused the loss; compliance and contracting requirements must be checked early and explicitly.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Manufacturing IT transformation at comparable managed-services scale. Supports mobilization, named-team, manufacturing-domain and trust-building win themes.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Domain depth, client trust and fastest mobilization—team ready within two weeks—helped win.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Larger than Kohler but relevant for landing-zone templates, migration factory tooling, automated discovery, security baselines, DR runbooks and other risk-reduction assets.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Proprietary IP reduced risk profile significantly; domain credentials and reusable accelerators helped win against Accenture and IBM.

similar_deals.title: Healthcare IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Different industry, but similar size/duration enterprise IT transformation. Direct warning against over-engineering and overpricing the downstream hosting/DC exit program.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost despite a better solution because pricing was 20% above the winning bid; commercial discipline and modular pricing are essential.

similar_deals.title: Telecom IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Different industry but similar enterprise IT transformation economics. Reinforces the need for run-rate transparency, migration unit pricing and optionality.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost because pricing was 20% above the winning bid despite a better solution; value must be measurable, not just technically superior.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 43%. Evidence is mixed and should not be overstated. Positive evidence: there is exact Kohler Project Polaris proposal intelligence with strong reusable positioning, delivery methodology and pricing bands; the deal corpus contains relevant manufacturing transformation wins including DEAL-003, DEAL-089, DEAL-038 and DEAL-094, showing credible patterns around SAP/manufacturing depth, rapid mobilization, proprietary accelerators and risk reduction. Negative evidence: there is no public procurement data, so competitor incumbency and market-clearing price cannot be validated; no provided evidence proves an existing Kohler relationship or incumbent advantage; the RFP has eliminatory compliance and DCaaS attestation requirements; comparable DEAL-056 was lost on a compliance appendix issue; and comparable transformation losses DEAL-043 and DEAL-029 show that being 20% above market can lose even with a better solution. Because procurement ground truth is unavailable and direct Kohler win history is not evidenced, the probability is held below 50%. Confidence: medium-low, based on two usable intelligence layers—past proposal content and deal corpus—with no public procurement validation.
capability_gaps: [MUST_HAVE: target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles] Past Kohler proposal content contains reusable landing-zone concepts, but the provided intelligence does not evidence a completed Kohler-specific architecture diagram or subscription/account model mapped to the inventory dataset; this must be produced, not left as narrative.

[MUST_HAVE: landing zone deployment model and application deployment model] The intelligence supports a 12-week Hosting Decision Lab and reference architecture approach, but does not prove that deployment patterns for each workload class—SAP, Oracle, SQL Server, Linux/Windows VMs, file storage, backup/DR, OT-adjacent integrations—are already defined for Kohler’s actual inventory.

[MUST_HAVE: hybrid connectivity approach] Prior content recommends Wisconsin DC-to-cloud/DCaaS connectivity, WAN/SD-WAN, private connectivity and VPN fallback, but no evidence is provided of current Kohler network topology, carrier constraints, latency requirements, plant connectivity dependencies, or circuit pricing validation.

[ELIMINATORY: evidence of certifications required] Deal corpus shows certifications in comparable deals, including ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, PCI-DSS L1 and Azure Gold, but the response must include current, entity-specific certification evidence applicable to the proposed hosting/DCaaS/cloud locations; missing attestations would be disqualifying.

[ELIMINATORY: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations] The intelligence does not confirm selected DCaaS facility certifications, audit reports, data center location attestations, or subcontractor compliance evidence for the Kohler solution; these must be obtained before submission.

[MUST_HAVE: use provided inventory dataset as sizing baseline] Past proposal pricing is inferred at $15M-$50M+ downstream and $279K-$595K for Phase 0, but no public procurement benchmark is available and the intelligence does not include actual Kohler inventory volumes, storage totals, utilization, backup retention, RTO/RPO tiers, or database sizes; pricing must be recalibrated to the dataset.

[MUST_HAVE: region strategy and data residency approach] The RFP requires region strategy and data residency, but the intelligence does not identify Kohler’s required data residency jurisdictions, plant/location constraints, or whether global workloads can be hosted from US regions/DCaaS facilities.
recommended_win_themes: Evidence-led Polaris Hosting Decision Lab: position the pursuit as a vendor-neutral workload-by-workload decision program across cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration, using USD-only open-book TCO. This directly reuses the strongest Kohler proposal pattern.

SAP-aware, manufacturing-safe data-center transition: emphasize SAP/non-SAP segmentation, database platform handling, low-disruption migration, rollback gates, DR tiers, and manufacturing operations continuity. This is supported by DEAL-003, where manufacturing SAP credibility and executive sponsor alignment were decisive.

Concrete secure landing zone, not generic cloud narrative: provide diagrams and deployment models for multi-account/multi-subscription architecture, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity integration, logging, monitoring, backup, DR, FinOps, and policy-as-code. This applies win lessons from DEAL-038 and DEAL-094 where proprietary IP and risk reduction mattered.

Transparent modular commercial model: separate Phase 0, foundation, migration, run, support, DR, security and optional enhancements with unit rates and volume bands. This directly addresses the pricing-loss lessons from DEAL-043 and DEAL-029.

Compliance-first bid discipline: make certifications, DCaaS attestations, US/USD contracting, data residency, confidentiality, subcontractor controls and response-template completeness visible in the executive response, not buried in appendices. This is required by the RFP and reinforced by DEAL-056 and DEAL-005 losses.
risk_factors: No public procurement award data is available, so there is no ground-truth validation of incumbent strength, winning price levels, or competitor win patterns for similar Kohler or Wisconsin manufacturing hosting contracts.

No evidence in the provided intelligence of an existing Kohler relationship or incumbent advantage; DEAL-094 shows prior relationship can be decisive, so absence of proof should depress win probability.
The RFP contains eliminatory compliance requirements; comparable DEAL-056 was lost because of a missed local entity/compliance appendix requirement despite strong fit.

Pricing risk is material: comparable transformation losses DEAL-043 and DEAL-029 were lost when pricing was 20% above the winning bid despite a better solution. A broad $15M-$50M downstream scope could invite over-engineering.

The requirement set is architecture-heavy; vague responses on landing zone deployment, application deployment, identity, hybrid connectivity, region/data residency, DR/HA, and Prod/Test/Dev segregation could make the bid non-comparable or weak.
DCaaS compliance attestations are explicitly mandatory; if a DCaaS partner or facility cannot provide current attestations, that option may be non-compliant.

Inventory quality risk: the RFP requires sizing and pricing based on the provided inventory dataset, but the intelligence notes likely data gaps. Unsupported assumptions could undermine commercial credibility.

Competitors likely include major infrastructure/cloud/system integrator firms; deal corpus shows competition from Accenture, Deloitte, TCS, Infosys, Capgemini, Atos, DXC, Fujitsu, IBM, Wipro and Cognizant in comparable transformations.

Phase ambiguity risk: the immediate RFP appears to be evaluation/advisory while the downstream hosting/DC exit is much larger. Mispricing Phase 0 as a full transformation or under-scoping it as a light assessment would both hurt competitiveness.
must_fix_before_bidding: Build a mandatory compliance matrix covering certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, US contracting entity, audit rights, subcontractor disclosure, data residency, confidentiality restrictions, USD pricing, proposal-template completeness, and submission-channel requirements; DEAL-056 and DEAL-005 show compliance appendix failures can lose otherwise strong bids.
Attach current ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, PCI-DSS where relevant, cloud partner, and DCaaS facility attestations for every proposed hosting option; do not rely on generic corporate claims.

Convert the Kohler inventory dataset into a sizing workbook with explicit assumptions for VMs, physical servers, SAP/non-SAP, SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL, storage, backup, DR, network, monitoring, logging and security tooling.

Produce concrete architecture artifacts: multi-account/multi-subscription landing zone, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity federation, hybrid connectivity, region/data residency, backup/DR, observability, security tooling, and operating model diagrams.

Price modularly in USD: Phase 0 advisory, foundation/landing zone, connectivity, migration waves, hosting/DCaaS/cloud consumption, backup/DR, monitoring/security, managed services, and optional transformation services; avoid a bundled premium bid given past losses where pricing was 20% above market.

Secure named manufacturing, SAP, VMware/data-center-exit, database, and US/North America references and assign a named Phase 0 team with a two-week mobilization commitment, reflecting the win lesson from DEAL-089.

Clarify whether the bid is for evaluation-only Phase 0, downstream hosting/DC exit, or both; keep Phase 0 pricing in the $279K-$595K evidence range unless the inventory scope materially justifies deviation.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: medium confidence. Accenture is a strong capability fit and has current infrastructure/cloud/data-center architect hiring plus Wisconsin-related activity, but I did not find a public Accenture job posting explicitly for Wisconsin hosting/DCaaS or a recent Wisconsin hosting contract win. Under the rule, this is not high likelihood. Sources: Accenture job posting/web intelligence; no public procurement data available; no recent contract wins in Wisconsin found.
competitors.predicted_positioning: Accenture will likely position as the premium global transformation partner: secure cloud-enabled enterprise operating model, standardized landing zones, AI-enabled infrastructure operations, manufacturing digital operations, and hyperscaler ecosystem depth. Evidence: NATO seven-year secure cloud-enabled digital infrastructure contract announced July 7, 2026; TEPCO five-year AI-led operations program announced June 3, 2026; Stellantis/NVIDIA manufacturing digital twin initiative announced May 18, 2026; Microsoft/Avanade agentic factory work announced April 20, 2026; Google Cloud Gemini partnership expansion announced April 22, 2026. Confidence: medium-high because multiple web sources align, but no Wisconsin-specific win was found.
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: Full program likely $26M-$50M over 3-5 years; Phase 0 likely premium-priced relative to smaller firms. Calculation: public procurement price anchors are unavailable. Using financial margin floor only: Q3 FY26 operating margin = 17.0%, so Accenture's sustainable price floor per $1.00 delivery cost is $1/(1-0.17) = $1.20; FY26 GAAP margin guidance 15.2%-15.4% implies $1.18 per $1.00 cost. Because the client-inferred opportunity is $15M-$50M and Accenture sells premium global managed services, I would expect them in the upper half unless they discount Phase 0 to capture the run program. Sources: Accenture Q3 FY26 earnings/web financial signal; no public procurement data available. Confidence: medium; no public contract values for this exact scope.
their_strengths: Very strong managed-services scale: Q3 FY26 managed services revenue was $9.39B and Q3 FY26 new bookings were $19.7B. Source: Accenture Q3 FY26 earnings/web financial signal.

Current secure enterprise infrastructure proof point: NATO seven-year Protected Business Network contract for secure cloud-enabled digital infrastructure. Source: Accenture July 7, 2026 news/web intelligence.
Strong manufacturing/digital-operations narrative from Stellantis/NVIDIA digital twins and Microsoft/Avanade agentic factory work. Source: Accenture web intelligence.

Cloud/infrastructure capabilities cover modernization, migration, hybrid/multi-cloud operating models, DR, monitoring/logging, security integration, and managed infrastructure services. Source: Accenture services and job posting web intelligence.
their_weaknesses: No public Accenture deal announcement found for a Wisconsin hosting/data-center-exit/DCaaS program matching Kohler's scope; no recent contract wins in Wisconsin found. Source: web intelligence/public procurement source.
Wisconsin-local delivery is not proven beyond Wisconsin-related activity and broad infrastructure roles; no explicit Wisconsin hosting/DCaaS hiring signal was found. Source: job posting intelligence.

Premium margin structure creates an opening for a lower, more transparent USD commercial model: Q3 FY26 operating margin 17.0% and FY26 GAAP margin guidance 15.2%-15.4%. Source: Accenture earnings/web financial signal.
how_to_beat_them: Force named Wisconsin/Midwest delivery staffing, not generic global capacity: the evidence shows Accenture has Wisconsin-related activity around Racine/Mount Pleasant/CNH and broad infrastructure roles, but no public Accenture Wisconsin hosting/DCaaS win or Wisconsin-specific hosting job posting was found. Source: web intelligence/job posting signal.

Neutralize their AI/manufacturing story by requiring the Phase 0 deliverable to stay focused on workload dispositioning, SAP/VMware dependencies, landing zones, DR, data residency, and USD TCO; Accenture will likely lean on Stellantis/NVIDIA digital twins, Microsoft/Avanade agentic factory, TEPCO AI-led operations, and Google Cloud Gemini messaging. Source: Accenture web/news intelligence.

Attack price transparency: require line-item separation of advisory, migration factory, hosting, cloud pass-through, tooling, run services, exit assistance, and indexation caps. Their Q3 FY26 operating margin was 17.0% and FY26 GAAP margin guidance was 15.2%-15.4%, so they must preserve premium economics. Source: Accenture Q3 FY26 earnings/web financial signal.

Require concrete data-center-exit references for VMware, physical servers, SAP ecosystem hosting, backup/DR, and hybrid connectivity. Raw live cloud subtotal is therefore $3.293M, expanded to $5.400M for enterprise networking, private connectivity, backup/DR, OS/middleware/database licensing, security tooling, monitoring integration, and DCaaS/hybrid connectivity layers. Confidence is moderate because pricing is strongly supported by past Kohler proposal anchors and live cloud unit economics, but actual Kohler inventory volumes, storage, utilization, RTO/RPO tiers, circuit pricing, and DCaaS facility quotes must still be validated.

draft.executive_summary: Project Polaris is not a binary cloud-versus-data-center exercise. It is a resilience, cost, architecture, security and operating-model decision for a global manufacturer whose Wisconsin data center, plants, SAP landscape, digital operations and customer experiences must continue to perform while hosting options are compared. Kohler's January 2026 technology priorities, as publicly described by its CDO, emphasize operational excellence, productivity, customer value, business responsiveness through platforms, digital engineering and domain expertise. The August 2025 CDO remit also points to scaling data, analytics, AI, foundational platforms and cybersecurity. Polaris should therefore create confidence, not another provider dependency. We propose an evidence-led, vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab that compares public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration workload by workload using USD-only open-book TCO. Kohler will own the outputs: landing-zone reference designs, Terraform or Bicep starter code, dependency graphs, SAP and VMware disposition model, DR tiering, security evidence matrix, migration waves, rollback gates and a downstream package any provider can compete. This makes Polaris a decision factory, not a sales funnel, and gives Kohler a practical path to modernization without sacrificing manufacturing continuity or governance control.
win_themes: Evidence-led Polaris Hosting Decision Lab with vendor-neutral workload-by-workload decisions and USD-only open-book TCO.
SAP-aware, manufacturing-safe data-center transition with low-disruption migration, rollback gates and continuity protection.
Concrete secure landing zone with identity, logging, monitoring, backup, DR, FinOps and policy-as-code.
Transparent modular commercial model separating Phase 0, foundation, migration, run, support, DR, security and optional enhancements.
Compliance-first bid discipline covering certifications, DCaaS attestations, US/USD contracting, data residency, confidentiality and subcontractor controls.

sections.section_title: Target state architecture, security and governance capabilities
sections.content: The target state will be designed as a Kohler-owned architecture, not a provider-owned dependency. Phase 0 will produce reference designs for cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration patterns, with workload placement driven by dependency, latency, data gravity, security, cost and operating risk. The landing-zone model will define multi-account or multi-subscription segregation for production, test and development; identity federation with Kohler directories; privileged access; policy-as-code; logging; monitoring; backup; secrets management; patching; encryption; vulnerability management; and FinOps guardrails. SAP and database platforms will be segmented from commodity Windows, Linux and VMware workloads so manufacturing-critical systems receive explicit performance, resilience and rollback treatment. Governance will include a shared-responsibility matrix across Kohler, incumbent providers, cloud, DCaaS and optional run partners, plus a compliance evidence matrix covering certifications, data residency, confidentiality and subcontractor controls. The win theme is control through evidence: Kohler receives portable designs, IaC starters and decision records that can be executed by any qualified provider.

sections.section_title: Migration approach, risk considerations and transition strategy
sections.content: Our transition strategy starts with a no-regret evidence baseline before any workload is moved. The 12-week Hosting Decision Lab will inventory applications, servers, databases, storage, backup, licenses, integrations, service levels, contracts and operational ownership, then create dependency graphs across SAP, VMware/VxRail, manufacturing systems, customer channels, analytics platforms and plant connectivity. Each workload will receive a disposition: retain, rehost, replatform, refactor, replace with SaaS, move to DCaaS, relocate to plant/edge or defer. Migration waves will be sequenced by business criticality, dependency clusters, data volume, outage tolerance, compliance and cutover risk. For SAP and databases, we will define rehearsal cycles, validation scripts, performance baselines, backup checkpoints, dual-run criteria and rollback gates before production cutover. Incumbent coexistence will be managed through a vendor RACI, weekly risk board, change calendar and decision log. The win theme is manufacturing-safe modernization: Kohler can pursue hybrid migration only where the evidence shows lower risk and better value.

Networking will be treated as a hosting decision input, not an afterthought. Phase 0 will map WAN, internet, plant, DC, cloud, SaaS, remote access and third-party connectivity paths, including latency-sensitive SAP, database, manufacturing and analytics flows. Target designs will compare direct cloud interconnects, DCaaS cross-connects, SD-WAN segmentation, zero-trust access, DNS, IP address management, firewall policy, load balancing and egress controls. We will identify single points of failure, bandwidth constraints, inspection choke points and data-sovereignty considerations before recommending placement. Connectivity patterns will be costed in the same USD TCO workbook as compute, storage, licenses, support and DR, so Kohler can see the full economic impact of hybrid design choices. The win theme is evidence-led neutrality: network architecture will support cloud, DCaaS, retained DC and plant/edge options without forcing a premature provider decision.

sections.section_title: Availability, resilience and disaster recovery approach
sections.content: Availability and disaster recovery will be defined by business impact, not generic uptime tiers. During Phase 0, we will classify applications and platforms by RTO, RPO, revenue or production impact, regulatory sensitivity, dependency chain and recovery complexity. The target model will define DR tiers for SAP, databases, identity, network services, manufacturing operations, analytics, customer-facing systems and collaboration platforms. For each tier, we will compare active-active, active-passive, backup-restore, DCaaS recovery, cloud-native replication and retained-site options, including runbook maturity and test frequency. Backup and immutable recovery controls will be aligned with cybersecurity requirements, privileged access and logging so resilience is not separated from security. Migration waves will include rehearsal, failback and rollback criteria before production cutover. The win theme is manufacturing continuity: Kohler receives a risk-priced resilience model that protects critical operations while avoiding over-engineering lower-risk workloads.

Polaris economics must be defensible to technology, finance and business operations. We will build a USD-only, open-book 3-5 year TCO workbook covering current run cost, DCaaS, public cloud, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration scenarios. The model will separate Phase 0, foundation, migration, run, support, DR, security, connectivity, licensing and optional enhancements, with unit rates, volume bands, assumptions, exclusions and sensitivity ranges visible to Kohler. Cloud costs will include compute, storage, database, backup, observability, security tooling, data transfer, support and reserved or savings-plan assumptions; DCaaS costs will include facility, hardware, managed operations, cross-connects, remote hands, recovery and exit obligations. The commercial package will not require a proprietary managed-services platform. The win theme is transparent optionality: Kohler can compete Phase 0 outputs among providers and choose the hosting path with the clearest value-risk trade-off.

The operating model will define who owns each control, service and decision before transition begins. Phase 0 will map current accountabilities across Kohler teams, incumbent providers, cloud, DCaaS, security, network, application, SAP, database and workplace support functions. The target RACI will cover incident, problem, change, release, capacity, patching, backup, DR testing, vulnerability remediation, access approvals, FinOps, service reporting and continual improvement. We will design a co-managed model that allows Kohler to keep strategic architecture, security governance and vendor choice while using provider capabilities where they improve resilience or cost. Support will include escalation paths, service review cadence, knowledge transfer, runbook standards, hypercare entry and exit criteria, and measures tied to availability, performance and business impact. The win theme is governed coexistence: Polaris complements the current Cognizant ecosystem by creating clear decision rights, measurable service outcomes and portable operating documentation.

Tooling will support decision quality and future operations without creating lock-in. The lab will use discovery, dependency mapping, cloud assessment, CMDB, contract, cost, vulnerability and observability inputs to create a reconciled workload evidence base. Target designs will specify Terraform or Bicep IaC starters, policy-as-code, CI/CD guardrails, configuration baselines, monitoring, log aggregation, AIOps event correlation, backup orchestration, patch automation and service reporting patterns. Where Kohler already has preferred tools or incumbent platforms, we will integrate rather than replace unless the evidence shows functional, security or cost gaps. The ecosystem comparison will remain neutral across public cloud, DCaaS, hybrid, retained data center and SaaS models, with Azure and data-gravity implications evaluated explicitly. The win theme is mature tooling with buyer ownership: Kohler receives reusable artifacts, not a dependency on an opaque provider platform.

We will align deliverables to Kohler's evaluation need: a defensible hosting recommendation and an execution-ready path. Phase 0 outputs will include the current-state baseline, application/server/database dependency graph, SAP/VMware disposition model, workload placement scorecards, target-state architecture, security and compliance evidence matrix, network and DR designs, migration roadmap, rollback plan, vendor coexistence RACI, operating model, risk register and USD 3-5 year TCO workbook. Each deliverable will have an owner, acceptance criteria, source evidence, assumption log and executive summary so bid teams, finance, security, infrastructure and business stakeholders can validate the recommendation. The final package will also include portable landing-zone designs and downstream RFP or SOW material that any qualified provider can execute. The win theme is response completeness with no hidden dependency: Kohler gets decision-ready evidence and implementation-ready artifacts.

pursuit_plan.strategy: Pursue this as a strategic Phase 0 advisory-led infrastructure transformation opportunity that positions the bidder to own the future data center exit or hybrid hosting program. The response should be option-neutral, evidence-heavy, and commercially transparent, giving Kohler a defensible comparison of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid target states while demonstrating readiness for secure execution at enterprise manufacturing scale.
pursuit_plan.win_strategy: Win by becoming Kohler's trusted, option-neutral decision partner: provide the clearest risk-adjusted comparison of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid architectures with mandatory compliance evidence, transparent USD economics, and a practical migration path from the Wisconsin data center to a resilient global operating model.
pursuit_plan.pricing_approach: Use a hybrid pricing strategy: transparent cost-plus detail for baseline hosting, DCaaS, managed services, connectivity, tooling, backup/DR, and support; competitive benchmarking for commodity infrastructure and migration factory rates; and value-based framing for risk reduction, data center exit enablement, resilience, governance, and future operating-model benefits. Provide separate USD price models for Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid scenarios, plus a distinct Phase 0 advisory price, with itemized assumptions, optional services, one-time versus recurring charges, consumption sensitivities, committed-use/reserved-capacity options, licensing treatment, and net 90 commercial accommodation where feasible.
key_risks: Attachment risk: the Evaluation Template, Vendor Response Template, Q&A template, and Inventory Baseline Dataset are missing from the provided RFP summary; hidden scoring weights, mandatory fields, certification requirements, inventory assumptions, and eliminators may exist there.

Compliance evidence risk: certifications are mandatory but not named in the main document, so the pursuit must identify required attestations and provide proof for cloud, DCaaS, and any third-party hosting locations.

Proposal-completeness risk: incomplete template responses, bare Yes/No answers, missed assumptions, non-USD pricing, late submission, or wrong communication channel could cause exclusion regardless of solution quality.

Pricing comparability risk: Kohler wants transparent and comparable pricing across Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid scenarios, but the future award scope is undefined; poor separation of Phase 0, migration, hosting, support, and optional costs may reduce credibility.

Inventory-quality risk: sizing, migration cost, DR design, and hosting economics depend on the inventory baseline, which may have gaps in dependencies, utilization, licensing, database versions, SAP sizing, storage I/O, network flows, and OT-adjacent constraints.

Strategic ambiguity risk: the RFP is Phase 0 evaluation-only but asks for pricing across hosting, migration, consulting, support, and managed services; overcommitting to implementation details or under-detailing future execution could both be penalized.
Competitive risk: infrastructure incumbents or existing Kohler cloud/SAP partners may have superior environment knowledge, pricing leverage, or pre-existing stakeholder relationships.
Commercial risk: net 90 payment terms are described as competitive, and failure to accommodate them may weaken the proposal even if not formally eliminatory.
Solution-bias risk: a public-cloud-only or DCaaS-only answer may score poorly because Kohler explicitly wants evaluation of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options with clear assumptions.

OT/security risk: selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope only for read-only or controlled integration; any design implying uncontrolled OT integration could raise security and operational concerns.

gaps: Competitor intelligence is materially incomplete/truncated: only Accenture is visible, while likely decisive bidders/incumbents such as Cognizant, Kyndryl, HCLTech, TCS, Infosys, DXC, NTT, Equinix, Rackspace/Cloudreach, AWS/Azure partners, and DCaaS/colocation providers are not assessed.

Major incumbent signal is underused: Client intel says Kohler extended Cognizant for AI, cloud, infrastructure operations and DC ops. Cognizant should be treated as a high-probability incumbent competitor or ecosystem constraint, not just a background fact.

Competitor pricing is weakly grounded. Accenture price range is inferred mainly from overall operating margin and the RFP’s estimated deal size, not from comparable managed infrastructure/cloud migration/DCaaS contract values.

Win intelligence is partially circular: the top 'similar deal' is the same Kohler Polaris pursuit with no win/loss outcome, which is useful for reuse but not valid evidence for win-probability calibration.
No clear calibrated win probability is shown in the supplied WIN_INTEL excerpt, and capability gaps/disqualifier risks are not explicitly tied to probability.

Several cited client intelligence items are strong but need source verification because they rely on recent web/job-posting signals and future/current-year press items; proceed only if the source links were captured elsewhere in the pipeline.
Pricing realism check is not applicable yet because this is Phase 2, but future pricing must include advisory Phase 0 separately from downstream run/migration/hosting economics.
strengths: Client intelligence contains specific, proposal-useful signals rather than generic manufacturing trends: Cognizant extension, CDO remit, AI/data/cybersecurity, observability, ServiceNow, Azure/Databricks and network modernization.

Unstated client needs are mostly actionable: independent TCO model, hybrid migration roadmap, vendor governance, AIOps-ready operations, security-by-design, ServiceNow integration and network resilience.
RFP disqualifiers are clearly captured, especially compliance evidence, structured response completeness, detailed template completion, USD pricing, confidentiality and submission rules.
Win intel includes several relevant manufacturing transformation comparables with useful lessons around SAP, compliance, mobilization, domain depth and migration-risk reduction.

Competitor analysis for Accenture is better than generic: it cites relevant secure infrastructure, manufacturing digital operations and hyperscaler ecosystem proof points, though pricing evidence remains weak.

agentic_decisions.reasoning: Pursue this as a strategic Phase 0 advisory-led infrastructure transformation opportunity that positions the bidder to own the future data center exit or hybrid hosting program. The response should be option-neutral, evidence-heavy, and commercially transparent, giving Kohler a defensible comparison of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid target states while demonstrating readiness for secure execution at enterprise manufacturing scale.

Client intelligence is actionable and contains specific signals: Cognizant extension, CDO remit, AI/data/cybersecurity priorities, ServiceNow, Azure/Databricks, observability, network architecture, and hybrid hosting needs. However, the competitor view is not good enough for a winning proposal because it appears incomplete and misses the most strategically important competitor: Cognizant, the apparent incumbent/expanded partner for cloud, infrastructure, DC ops, AI and cybersecurity. Accenture analysis has useful capability signals but pricing is not grounded in comparable deal evidence. WIN_INTEL has relevant manufacturing transformation comparables, but using the same Kohler pursuit as the highest-similarity 'similar deal' weakens calibration. These gaps materially affect win strategy, pricing, differentiation, and incumbent-displacement narrative, so the pipeline should deepen/re-run targeted agents before proceeding.
